docs.title: Flox
docs.text:
Flox is a modular C++ framework for building low-latency execution systems. It provides a clean separation between abstract interfaces and optimized implementations, allowing for:

  • Real-time trading infrastructure
  • Event-driven processing
  • Simulation and live execution
  • Deterministic performance tuning with full control over memory and routing

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• Pluggable connectors and routing
  • Fast in-memory order books
  • Strategy engine with lifecycle control
  • Unified subscription bus for market data
  • Dedicated buses for book, trade, candle, and order events
  • Generic EventBus powers all event dispatch

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Flox is a modular framework for building low-latency execution systems. Its design emphasizes separation of concerns, predictable performance, and composability.

docs.title: 1. Abstract Layer
docs.text:
Defines pure interfaces with no internal state. These are the contracts your system is built upon:

  • IStrategy: strategy logic
  • IOrderExecutor: order submission
  • IOrderExecutionListener: execution events
  • IRiskManager, IOrderValidator, IPositionManager: trade controls and state
  • IOrderBook, ExchangeConnector: market structure
  • ISubsystem: unified lifecycle interface
  • IMarketDataSubscriber: receives events via data bus

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
• Enables simulation, replay, and mocking
  • Decouples logic from implementation
  • Ensures correctness can be validated independently of performance

docs.title: 2. Implementation Layer
docs.text:
• Engine — core coordinator implementing IEngine
  • FullOrderBook — full-depth order storage for all price levels and quantities
  • WindowedOrderBook — tick-centric view with center/tick size logic
  • BookUpdateEvent, TradeEvent — pooled and reusable market data events
  • EventPool + EventHandle — zero-allocation event lifecycle manager
  • MarketDataBus — fan-out with lock-free queues (SPSCQueue)
  • EventBus — generic template used by all bus types
  • SPSCQueue — bounded ring buffer for 1:1 messaging
  • RefCountable — atomic reference counting for pooled objects
  • SymbolRegistry — maps strings to SymbolId
  • CandleAggregator — rolling OHLC aggregator
  • SimpleOrderExecutor — minimal executor used in the demo
  • PositionManager — tracks open positions
  • Subsystem<T> — wraps non-subsystem modules for uniform lifecycle

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Speed: memory locality, std::pmr, lock-free queues
  • Control: minimal allocations, deterministic fan-out
  • Modularity: no global state, explicit composition

docs.title: Strategy Execution: PUSH and PULL Modes
docs.text: Strategies implement IMarketDataSubscriber and can operate in two modes:

docs.title: PUSH Mode (default)
docs.text:
The bus actively delivers events to the strategy.

class MyPushStrategy : public IStrategy {
public:
  void onBookUpdate(BookUpdateEvent *event) override { /* handle event */ }
};

The engine subscribes the strategy via:

marketDataBus->subscribe(strategy);

docs.title: PULL Mode
docs.text:
The strategy explicitly pulls from its queue (used in polling setups or backtests):

class MyPullStrategy : public IMarketDataSubscriber {
public:
  SubscriberMode mode() const override { return SubscriberMode::PULL; }

  void readLoop(SPSCQueue<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>> &queue) {
    EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent> ev;
    while (queue.pop(ev)) {
      EventDispatcher<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>>::dispatch(ev, *this);
    }
  }
};

docs.title: Market Data Fan-Out: MarketDataBus
docs.text: The MarketDataBus delivers EventHandle<T> to each subscriber via dedicated lock-free queues.

docs.title: Event publishing:
docs.text: bus->publish(std::move(bookUpdate));

docs.title: Fan-out process
docs.text:
• Each subscriber has a queue (SPSCQueue)
  • EventHandle<T> wraps and manages event lifecycle
  • EventDispatcher delivers each event to the subscriber's callback

docs.title: Subscribing:
docs.text:
bus->subscribe(myStrategy);

In sync mode, subscribers are coordinated via TickBarrier and TickGuard.

docs.title: Lifecycle and Subsystems
docs.text:
Each runtime component (strategy, book, sink) is a ISubsystem or wrapped in Subsystem<T>. They expose start() and stop() for controlled startup/shutdown.

This enables:

  • Replay testing
  • Safe teardown
  • Deterministic benchmarking

docs.title: Memory and Performance
docs.text:
Flox emphasizes allocation-free execution paths:

  • BookUpdateEvent and TradeEvent come from pooled memory
  • EventHandle<T> ensures safe scoped lifecycle
  • SPSCQueue supports lock-free delivery
  • std::pmr::vector is used for zero-allocation order book updates

docs.title: Symbol-Centric Design
docs.text:
Every operation is indexed by SymbolId — a compact uint32_t mapped from exchange:symbol:

  • No string hashing or comparison in hot paths
  • Fast per-symbol routing, filtering, fan-out
  • Compatible with routing layers (SymbolIdOrderRouter, etc.)

docs.title: Intended Use
docs.text:
Flox is not a complete trading engine. It is a toolbox for building:

  • Real-time trading systems
  • Simulators and backtesters
  • Distributed signal routers
  • Custom HFT infrastructure

Perfect for C++ teams needing:

  • Predictable latency
  • Fine-grained memory control
  • Composable systems

docs.title: Example Integration
docs.text:
auto strategy = std::make_shared<MyStrategy>();
marketDataBus->subscribe(strategy);

marketDataBus->publish(std::move(bookUpdate));

In pull-mode:

auto *queue = marketDataBus->getQueue(strategy->id());
while (queue->pop(event)) {
  EventDispatcher<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>>::dispatch(event, *strategy);
}

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Flox is:

  • Modular — you compose what you need
  • Predictable — designed for deterministic latency
  • Safe — pooled events, scoped handles, lifecycle management
  • Flexible — works in simulation, backtest, or live modes

You define your logic — Flox moves the data.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: This roadmap outlines upcoming improvements and tooling for the Flox HFT Engine.

docs.title: 0. Developer Experience
docs.text: • Formatting tools

docs.title: 1. Binary Format for Market Data
docs.text:
• Efficient serialization format for storing market data snapshots and trades.
  • Designed to be faster and more compact than JSON or CSV.
  • Intended for use in both live systems and backtesting environments.

docs.title: 2. Backtesting Tooling
docs.text:
• Infrastructure for offline strategy evaluation.
  • Will support:
  • Market data replay (from binary format).
  • Order simulation and execution environment.
  • Strategy performance statistics and visualization.

docs.title: 3. Integrated JS engine
docs.text: • To allow writing strategies using JavaScript

docs.title: Long-Term Goals
docs.text:
• Keep the engine modular and extensible.
  • Ensure compatibility between live trading and backtest environments.

docs.title: Order Book Benchmark Results
docs.text:
These benchmark results compare the performance of the two order book implementations in Flox:

  • FullOrderBook: a general-purpose order book based on std::map
  • WindowedOrderBook: a performance-optimized order book with fixed-size price windows and ring buffers

All benchmarks were run on:

  • CPU: 8 x 4200 MHz
  • Cache: L1d 48 KiB, L1i 32 KiB (x4), L2 1280 KiB (x4), L3 8192 KiB (x1)
  • Load average: ~1.6
  • CPU scaling: enabled

⚠️ Note: CPU scaling may affect measurements. Real-time results may incur extra overhead.

docs.title: Engine Interface
docs.text: The IEngine interface defines the top-level runtime control for the Flox trading system.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide lifecycle management for the entire engine, including startup and shutdown sequences.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class IEngine {
public:
  virtual ~IEngine() = default;
  virtual void start() = 0;
  virtual void stop() = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Initialize and start all registered subsystems (strategies, bus, sink, etc.)
  • Control shutdown procedure for graceful termination

docs.title: Implementations
docs.text: • Constructed via IEngineBuilder

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Entry point for running Flox in production or demo mode
  • Should be triggered after system configuration and strategy wiring

docs.title: Abstract Engine Builder
docs.text: The IEngineBuilder interface defines the contract for constructing the Flox engine and its subsystems.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To allow flexible configuration and construction of an IEngine instance with all required subsystems and strategies.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IEngineBuilder {
public:
  virtual ~IEngineBuilder() = default;

  virtual std::unique_ptr<IEngine> build() = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Encapsulate the logic of creating and wiring together all engine components
  • Provide a customizable way to build different configurations of the engine

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Typically used in main application code to construct the runtime engine
  • Ensures separation of construction and runtime execution phases

docs.title: Event Pool Interface
docs.text: The IEventPool interface defines a generic contract for releasing market data events back into a memory pool.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To allow efficient reuse of market data event objects (e.g., BookUpdateEvent, TradeEvent) by avoiding repeated allocations and deallocations.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IEventPool {
public:
  virtual ~IEventPool() = default;
  virtual void release(IMarketDataEvent *event) = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Provide a mechanism to return IMarketDataEvent instances to the pool
  • Act as a common base for all type-specific event pools

docs.title: Integration
docs.text:
• Typically used with EventHandle<T> to manage scoped lifetime
  • EventHandle automatically releases the event back to the IEventPool when it goes out of scope

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used by MarketDataBus and MarketDataEventPool
  • Ensures event lifecycle is managed outside of consumers
  • Enables zero-allocation data pipelines

docs.title: Order Execution Listener
docs.text: The IOrderExecutionListener interface allows components to receive notifications when an order is filled or rejected.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This interface is used to propagate execution events to listeners, typically for updating internal state or triggering follow-up logic.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IOrderExecutionListener {
public:
  virtual ~IOrderExecutionListener() = default;
  virtual void onOrderFilled(const Order &order) = 0;
  virtual void onOrderRejected(const Order &order, const std::string &reason) = 0;
};

docs.title: Typical Implementations
docs.text:
• PositionManager: updates symbol positions upon order fills
  • Custom loggers or analytics modules: record order results

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Can be used with MultiExecutionListener to dispatch to multiple consumers
  • Order rejections can include additional metadata via the reason string

docs.title: Execution Tracker
docs.text: The IExecutionTracker interface is responsible for tracking the lifecycle of orders in the system with high-resolution timestamps.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This interface enables components to collect precise latency metrics and audit trails for order submissions, fills, and rejections.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IExecutionTracker {
public:
  virtual ~IExecutionTracker() = default;

  virtual void onOrderSubmitted(const Order &order,
                                std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderAccepted(const Order &order,
                               std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderPartiallyFilled(const Order &order, Quantity qty,
                                      std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderFilled(const Order &order,
                             std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderCanceled(const Order &order,
                               std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderExpired(const Order &order,
                              std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderRejected(const Order &order, const std::string &reason,
                               std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;

  virtual void onOrderReplaced(const Order &oldOrder, const Order &newOrder,
                               std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point ts) = 0;
};

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Measuring round-trip latency from order submission to execution
  • Diagnosing slow fills or rejections
  • Auditing execution behavior across multiple venues

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Timestamps are based on std::chrono::steady_clock to ensure monotonicity
  • Can be combined with ExecutionTrackerAdapter for flexible dispatching

docs.title: Order Executor
docs.text: The IOrderExecutor is the central interface for submitting and managing orders in the Flox framework. It acts as the bridge between the strategy layer and the actual execution venue or simulator.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Accepts and submits orders
  • Notifies listeners when orders are filled or rejected
  • Optionally tracks execution metrics via a tracker
  • Integrates into the system lifecycle via ISubsystem

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class IOrderExecutor : public ISubsystem {
public:
  virtual ~IOrderExecutor() = default;

  virtual void submitOrder(const Order &order) = 0;

  void setExecutionTracker(IExecutionTracker *tracker);
  void setListener(IOrderExecutionListener *listener);

protected:
  IExecutionTracker *getExecutionTracker() const;
  IOrderExecutionListener *getListener() const;
};

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• Strategies call submitOrder(...) to execute trades.
  • setListener(...) must be used to register a consumer for order events.
  • Optionally, setExecutionTracker(...) can be used to collect latency metrics.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Execution logic is implemented in derived classes (e.g., mock or real-time executors).
  • Listener and tracker pointers are nullable; behavior should be guarded accordingly.

docs.title: Kill Switch
docs.text: The IKillSwitch interface provides a mechanism for detecting critical execution conditions and halting trading activity.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A kill switch is used to protect the system from cascading failures, excessive losses, or abnormal behavior by forcibly stopping order flow.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IKillSwitch {
public:
  virtual ~IKillSwitch() = default;

  virtual void check(const Order &order) = 0;
  virtual void trigger(const std::string &reason) = 0;
  virtual bool isTriggered() const = 0;
  virtual std::string reason() const = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• check(...) allows pre-trade validation for dangerous or outlier orders
  • trigger(...) forcibly activates the kill switch with a descriptive reason
  • isTriggered() returns current status
  • reason() provides context for the shutdown

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Manual or automatic shutdowns under emergency
  • Circuit breakers for latency spikes, volume anomalies, or rejected order bursts
  • Integration with IOrderExecutor or IRiskManager

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The kill switch is a critical risk control tool in production systems
  • It is expected to be queried before submitting any order

docs.title: Market Data Subscriber
docs.text: The IMarketDataSubscriber interface defines the contract for components that receive market data from the bus.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To enable decoupled and efficient delivery of market data (e.g., book updates, trades) to consumers such as strategies or aggregators.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IMarketDataSubscriber : public ISubscriber {
public:
  virtual ~IMarketDataSubscriber() = default;

  virtual void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent &ev) {}
  virtual void onTrade(const TradeEvent &ev) {}
  virtual void onCandle(const CandleEvent &ev) {}
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Consume events dispatched from the book, trade, and candle buses
  • Identify each subscriber with a unique id()
  • Indicate whether it operates in PUSH or PULL mode

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• SubscriberId is used to track and route messages
  • Default mode is PUSH; override mode() to return PULL when polling is preferred

docs.title: Order Book Interface
docs.text: The IOrderBook interface defines the minimal contract for any order book implementation within Flox. It supports applying market data updates and querying top-of-book and level-specific liquidity.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To abstract the representation and access to order book state, allowing different implementations (e.g., full depth, windowed).

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IOrderBook {
public:
  virtual ~IOrderBook() = default;

  virtual void applyBookUpdate(const BookUpdate &update) = 0;
  virtual std::optional<double> bestBid() const = 0;
  virtual std::optional<double> bestAsk() const = 0;

  virtual double bidAtPrice(double price) const = 0;
  virtual double askAtPrice(double price) const = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• applyBookUpdate(...): update internal state with snapshot or delta updates
  • bestBid() / bestAsk(): return top-of-book prices, or std::nullopt if empty
  • bidAtPrice(...) / askAtPrice(...): return quantity available at a given price level

docs.title: Implementations
docs.text:
• FullOrderBook: full-depth representation
  • WindowedOrderBook: limited tick-range book for performance-sensitive applications

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Implementations must ensure thread safety if used in concurrent environments
  • Used directly by strategies and execution components
  • Implementations should inline performance-critical methods for optimal performance.

docs.title: Order Validator
docs.text: The IOrderValidator interface defines a contract for validating orders before submission. It is intended to enforce basic or complex sanity checks to prevent erroneous or harmful trades.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To ensure that orders meet criteria such as: - Minimum quantity - Price range limits - Symbol-specific constraints

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IOrderValidator {
public:
  virtual ~IOrderValidator() = default;

  virtual bool validate(const Order &order, std::string &reason) const = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text: • validate(...): inspects the order and returns true if valid. If false, the reason string is populated with the validation failure message.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Plugged into the order pipeline before execution
  • Risk-limiting logic for rogue order prevention
  • Strategy-specific restrictions (e.g., price bands, notional value limits)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Validation does not modify the order
  • Stateless implementations are preferred for thread safety

docs.title: PnL Tracker
docs.text: The IPnLTracker interface tracks realized profit and loss based on executed orders. It is designed to be notified whenever an order is filled, allowing cumulative performance accounting.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To measure realized profitability in trading strategies or the entire system.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IPnLTracker {
public:
  virtual ~IPnLTracker() = default;
  virtual void onOrderFilled(const Order &order) = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Receives every filled order via onOrderFilled(...)
  • Computes net PnL based on position changes, prices, and fees (if applicable)
  • May support symbol-wise aggregation or global totals

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Strategy-level performance reporting
  • Centralized analytics dashboards
  • Integration with monitoring or alerting systems

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This interface is intentionally minimal
  • Internal state (e.g., positions or entry prices) is managed by the implementation

docs.title: Position Manager Interface
docs.text: The IPositionManager interface provides access to position information per symbol. It is typically implemented by a component that tracks fills and updates internal position state.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To allow strategies, trackers, or execution components to query current exposure levels per trading symbol.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IPositionManager {
public:
  virtual ~IPositionManager() = default;

  virtual double getPosition(SymbolId symbol) const = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• getPosition(...): returns the current net position for the specified symbol
  • Underlying implementation must keep this data consistent and accurate

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Risk checks before submitting new orders
  • Displaying strategy exposure
  • Calculating PnL or exposure-weighted metrics

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This interface only provides read access
  • Actual position tracking is done by components like PositionManager

docs.title: Risk Manager
docs.text: The IRiskManager interface defines the logic responsible for enforcing trading risk limits before order submission.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To act as a gatekeeper for outbound orders, ensuring compliance with capital, exposure, and custom risk rules.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IRiskManager : public ISubsystem {
public:
  virtual ~IRiskManager() = default;
  virtual bool allow(const Order &order) const = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• allow(...): evaluates the order and returns true if it's safe to proceed
  • Integrated into the system lifecycle via ISubsystem

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Capital-based order blocking
  • Exposure control per symbol or globally
  • Custom constraints (e.g., time-of-day, user-defined logic)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Typically used in conjunction with IOrderExecutor
  • Stateless or stateful implementations depending on enforcement logic

docs.title: Strategy Interface
docs.text: The IStrategy interface defines the base class for implementing trading strategies in Flox. It combines market data subscription, lifecycle management, and dependency injection for execution, risk, and validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To allow developers to create modular strategies that respond to market data and place orders via injected subsystems.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class IStrategy : public IMarketDataSubscriber
{
 public:
  // Lifecycle
  virtual void onStart();
  virtual void onStop();

  // Event hooks
  virtual void onCandle(const CandleEvent& candle) override;
  virtual void onTrade(const TradeEvent& trade) override;
  virtual void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent& bookUpdate) override;

  SubscriberId id() const override;
  SubscriberMode mode() const override;

  // Dependency injection
  void setRiskManager(IRiskManager* manager);
  void setPositionManager(IPositionManager* manager);
  void setOrderExecutor(IOrderExecutor* executor);
  void setOrderValidator(IOrderValidator* validator);
  void setKillSwitch(IKillSwitch* killSwitch);
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Implements IMarketDataSubscriber with internal dispatching to typed events
  • Owns a unique SubscriberId for queue-based delivery
  • Reacts to market data with event handlers (onTrade, onBookUpdate, onCandle)
  • Lifecycle hooks (onStart, onStop) are optional

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• By default, subscribes in PUSH mode to MarketDataBus
  • All dependencies are injected via setter methods
  • Order flow can be guarded by validator, risk manager and killswitch before execution

docs.title: Event Dispatch
docs.text:
Events are delivered via dedicated callbacks without runtime type checks:

void onCandle(const CandleEvent& candle) override {
  // handle candle
}

void onTrade(const TradeEvent &trade) override {
  // handle trade
}

void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent &update) override {
  // handle book
}

docs.title: Example Order Submission
docs.text:
if (GetOrderValidator() && !GetOrderValidator()->validate(order, reason)) return;
if (GetRiskManager() && !GetRiskManager()->allow(order)) return;
GetOrderExecutor()->submitOrder(order);

docs.title: ArenaResource
docs.text: ArenaResource is a custom bump allocator that implements the C++ polymorphic memory resource interface (std::pmr::memory_resource). It allows high-performance memory allocation from a fixed-size buffer with an optional upstream fallback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide an efficient, low-latency allocator with predictable allocation patterns for performance-critical components.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class ArenaResource : public std::pmr::memory_resource {
public:
  ArenaResource(void *buffer, std::size_t size,
                std::pmr::memory_resource *upstream = std::pmr::null_memory_resource());

  void reset() noexcept;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Allocates memory linearly from a pre-allocated buffer ("bump allocator")
  • Falls back to upstream allocator if capacity is exceeded
  • Does not support deallocation — memory is released only via reset()

docs.title: Internal Behavior
docs.text:
• do_allocate(...) aligns and reserves space within the buffer
  • do_deallocate(...) is a no-op
  • reset() resets the allocator to the beginning of the buffer

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• High-throughput components requiring many short-lived allocations
  • Preallocated memory pools for real-time or embedded applications
  • Unit testing or deterministic memory control

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Thread-unsafe by design (intended for single-threaded access)
  • Make sure the buffer remains valid for the allocator’s lifetime

docs.title: BookSide
docs.text: The BookSide class represents one side (bid or ask) of an order book. It is designed as a fixed-size, ring-buffer-style structure optimized for low-latency price level access and updates.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To efficiently maintain and manipulate one side of the limit order book, supporting rapid updates and best-level queries.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class BookSide {
public:
  enum class Side { Bid, Ask };

  BookSide(std::size_t windowSize, Side side, std::pmr::memory_resource *mem);

  void setLevel(std::size_t index, double qty);
  double getLevel(std::size_t index) const;

  void shift(int levels);
  void clear();

  std::optional<std::size_t> findBest() const;

  std::pmr::memory_resource *allocator() const;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• setLevel(index, qty): sets quantity at a specific price level index
  • getLevel(index): retrieves quantity at index
  • shift(levels): shifts the book window by a relative offset
  • clear(): resets all levels
  • findBest(): finds the best price level (highest bid or lowest ask)

docs.title: Internal Design
docs.text:
• Ring buffer backed by std::pmr::vector<double>
  • Offset-based indexing via ring(...)
  • Tracks best index to avoid scanning entire window

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Used internally in WindowedOrderBook and related components
  • Performance-sensitive scenarios requiring rapid top-of-book updates

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • Memory resource passed via constructor enables allocator injection

docs.title: BookUpdateBus
docs.text: BookUpdateBus delivers BookUpdateEvent objects to all subscribed market data consumers. It is based on the generic EventBus and supports synchronous or asynchronous dispatch.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
#ifdef USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS
using BookUpdateBus = EventBus<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>, SyncPolicy<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>>>;
#else
using BookUpdateBus = EventBus<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>, AsyncPolicy<EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent>>>;
#endif

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Fan-out book updates with minimal latency
  • Provide per-subscriber queues (SPSCQueue)
  • Assign tick sequence numbers to events

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used internally by MarketDataBus
  • Controlled by the USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS compile definition

docs.title: Book Update Event
docs.text: The BookUpdateEvent represents a snapshot or delta update to the order book, including both bids and asks.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To deliver order book updates to subscribers like order books, strategies, or analytics modules with minimal latency and no allocations.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
struct BookUpdateEvent : public IMarketDataEvent {
  SymbolId symbol;
  BookUpdateType type;
  std::pmr::vector<BookLevel> bids;
  std::pmr::vector<BookLevel> asks;
  std::chrono::system_clock::time_point timestamp;

  BookUpdateEvent(std::pmr::memory_resource *res);
};

docs.title: BookLevel Structure
docs.text:
struct BookLevel {
  double price;
  double quantity;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Represent current book state or changes (snapshot vs delta)
  • Contain bid/ask levels in memory-resource-backed containers
  • Delivered to subscribers via the book update bus

docs.title: Memory Optimization
docs.text:
• Uses std::pmr::vector for bids and asks to reduce allocation overhead
  • Allocator is provided during construction from the owning EventPool

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Produced by market data connectors or simulators
  • Consumed by WindowedOrderBook, FullOrderBook, strategies, etc.
  • Supports accurate timestamping via timestamp field

docs.title: Candle
docs.text: The Candle struct represents a time-based OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) candlestick. It is used to summarize trade data over a specific time interval.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide a compact, aggregated view of price and volume action within a time window.

docs.title: Struct Definition
docs.text:
struct Candle {
  double open = 0.0;
  double high = 0.0;
  double low = 0.0;
  double close = 0.0;
  double volume = 0.0;
  std::chrono::system_clock::time_point startTime;
  std::chrono::system_clock::time_point endTime;
};

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
• open: first trade price in the interval
  • high: highest trade price
  • low: lowest trade price
  • close: last trade price
  • volume: total traded volume
  • startTime: beginning of the time interval
  • endTime: end of the time interval

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Strategy input (e.g. momentum or mean-reversion logic)
  • Visualization and charting
  • Statistical modeling

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Time is tracked using std::chrono::system_clock
  • Typically constructed and emitted by the CandleAggregator

docs.title: CandleAggregator
docs.text: The CandleAggregator is a subsystem that aggregates trade data into time-based candlesticks. It emits complete Candle objects for each symbol at regular intervals via a user-defined callback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To convert streaming trade data into OHLCV candles for analysis, strategy input, or logging.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class CandleAggregator : public ISubsystem {
public:
  using CandleCallback = std::function<void(SymbolId, const Candle &)>;

  CandleAggregator(std::chrono::seconds interval, CandleCallback callback);

  void start() override;
  void stop() override;

  void onTrade(const Trade &trade);
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Receives trades via onTrade(...)
  • Aggregates OHLCV values within a time interval (_interval)
  • Calls the registered CandleCallback when a new candle is completed
  • Tracks per-symbol candle state internally
  • Publishes completed candles to CandleBus

docs.title: Internal Design
docs.text:
• Uses std::unordered_map<SymbolId, PartialCandle> to accumulate state
  • Candle emission is based on aligned timestamps (alignToInterval(...))
  • Implements ISubsystem for system lifecycle compatibility

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Used by strategies that rely on time-based candles (e.g., moving average strategies)
  • Enables plotting or historical export of candlestick data
  • Provides compact representation of market activity

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The component is stateless between restarts unless externally persisted
  • Candle timestamps are system-clock-based

docs.title: CandleBus
docs.text: CandleBus publishes completed candle information from CandleAggregator to interested subscribers. It uses the generic EventBus and can operate in synchronous or asynchronous mode.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
#ifdef USE_SYNC_CANDLE_BUS
using CandleBus = EventBus<CandleEvent, SyncPolicy<CandleEvent>>;
#else
using CandleBus = EventBus<CandleEvent, AsyncPolicy<CandleEvent>>;
#endif

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Deliver CandleEvent objects produced by CandleAggregator
  • Provide lock-free queues per subscriber
  • Assign a tick sequence number when publishing

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Independent from the market data bus
  • Controlled by the USE_SYNC_CANDLE_BUS compile definition

docs.title: Common Types
docs.text: This file defines basic enums and type aliases used throughout the Flox framework. These primitives standardize order semantics and symbol representation.

docs.title: OrderType
docs.text:
enum class OrderType { LIMIT, MARKET };
  • LIMIT: Order to be executed at a specific price or better
  • MARKET: Order to be executed immediately at the best available price

docs.title: Side
docs.text:
enum class Side { BUY, SELL };
  • BUY: Indicates a buy-side order or trade
  • SELL: Indicates a sell-side order or trade

docs.title: SymbolId
docs.text:
using SymbolId = uint32_t;
  • A compact, internal identifier used to reference trading symbols across all components.
  • Typically mapped from exchange:symbol strings using SymbolRegistry.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Order routing and strategy logic
  • Efficient mapping and lookup
  • Readable and strongly-typed API

docs.title: ConnectorFactory
docs.text: The ConnectorFactory is a registry for dynamically constructing ExchangeConnector instances by type. It supports runtime registration of connector implementations and symbol-specific instantiation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To decouple connector instantiation from hardcoded logic and enable flexible, pluggable exchange integration.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class ConnectorFactory {
public:
  using CreatorFunc = std::function<std::shared_ptr<ExchangeConnector>(const std::string &symbol)>;

  static ConnectorFactory &instance();

  void registerConnector(const std::string &type, CreatorFunc creator);
  std::shared_ptr<ExchangeConnector> createConnector(const std::string &type, const std::string &symbol) const;

private:
  ConnectorFactory() = default;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Registers connector creation functions keyed by string type (e.g. "bybit", "mock")
  • Creates new ExchangeConnector instances on demand using the symbol name
  • Acts as a singleton factory used during engine startup or reconfiguration

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Symbol-specific connector routing
  • Plugin-like architecture for exchange connectors
  • Dynamic or test-time substitution of connector logic

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The CreatorFunc lambda should capture and bind any necessary context
  • Returns nullptr if the requested type is not registered

docs.title: ConnectorManager
docs.text: The ConnectorManager is a utility class responsible for managing and orchestrating multiple ExchangeConnector instances. It handles startup, callback wiring, and centralized control of market data ingestion.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To centralize exchange connector lifecycle management and routing of book and trade data.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class ConnectorManager {
public:
  void registerConnector(std::shared_ptr<ExchangeConnector> connector);

  void startAll(std::function<void(const BookUpdate &)> onBookUpdate,
                std::function<void(const Trade &)> onTrade);
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Registers connectors by exchangeId
  • Wires unified BookUpdate and Trade callbacks to each connector
  • Starts all registered connectors

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Bootstrapping all active exchange streams at engine startup
  • Multiplexing book/trade events into a single processing pipeline
  • Test harnesses and connector orchestration

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Internally stores connectors in a std::map<std::string, std::shared_ptr<...>>
  • Logs connector starts to stdout
  • Expects connectors to support setCallbacks(...) and start()

docs.title:
Decimal

The Decimal<Tag, Scale, TickSize> template represents a fixed-point decimal number with compile-time scaling and optional tick size rounding. It is used to represent precise quantities like price or volume in trading systems, avoiding floating-point errors.
Template Parameters
docs.text:
template <typename Tag, int Scale, int64_t TickSize = 1>
class Decimal;
  • Tag: Empty struct type used to distinguish different Decimal types (e.g. PriceTag, VolumeTag)
  • Scale: Integer scale factor (e.g. 1_000_000 for 6 decimal places)
  • TickSize: Optional tick size used for rounding and discretization

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• Provide a safe and precise alternative to double in financial applications
  • Ensure that prices and quantities are stored as integers internally
  • Avoid runtime ambiguity between different uses of decimals (e.g., price vs quantity)

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
static constexpr Decimal fromDouble(double val);
static constexpr Decimal fromRaw(int64_t raw);
constexpr double toDouble() const;
constexpr int64_t raw() const;
constexpr Decimal roundToTick() const;

constexpr bool isZero() const;

constexpr Decimal operator+(Decimal d) const;
constexpr Decimal operator-(Decimal d) const;
constexpr Decimal &operator+=(const Decimal &other);
constexpr Decimal &operator-=(const Decimal &other);
constexpr auto operator<=>(const Decimal &) const = default;

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
• Internally stores the value as a raw integer (int64_t _raw)
  • fromDouble() converts a double to internal integer by multiplying with Scale and rounding
  • toDouble() converts back to double for display or logging
  • roundToTick() aligns the internal value to the nearest multiple of TickSize
  • isZero() checks if the internal value is exactly zero

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This type is constexpr-friendly and usable in low-latency paths
  • The Tag type is not used at runtime, but enforces type-safety at compile time
  • All operators and conversions are explicit to avoid accidental misuse

docs.title: Example Use
docs.text:
struct PriceTag {};
struct VolumeTag {};

using Price = Decimal<PriceTag, 1'000_000>;
using Volume = Decimal<VolumeTag, 1'000_000>;

Price p = Price::fromDouble(12345.67);
Volume v = Volume::fromDouble(0.005);

double pv = p.toDouble();
int64_t raw = v.raw();

docs.title: EngineConfig
docs.text: The EngineConfig struct defines the top-level configuration for the Flox engine. It includes exchange setups, risk parameters, and logging preferences.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide a unified and structured way to configure system behavior and enabled components at startup.

docs.title: Struct Definitions
docs.text:
struct SymbolConfig {
  std::string symbol;
  double tickSize;
  double expectedDeviation;
};

struct ExchangeConfig {
  std::string name;
  std::string type;
  std::vector<SymbolConfig> symbols;
};

struct KillSwitchConfig {
  double maxOrderQty = 10'000.0;
  double maxLoss = -1e6;
  int maxOrdersPerSecond = -1;
};

struct EngineConfig {
  std::vector<ExchangeConfig> exchanges;
  KillSwitchConfig killSwitchConfig;

  std::string logLevel = "info";
  std::string logFile;
};

docs.title: Exchanges
docs.text:
• Each ExchangeConfig describes one exchange to connect:
  • name: logical identifier
  • type: connector type (e.g. "mock", "bybit")
  • symbols: list of SymbolConfig describing symbols and tick parameters

docs.title: KillSwitchConfig
docs.text:
• maxOrderQty: hard limit on order size
  • maxLoss: max PnL drawdown before triggering kill switch
  • maxOrdersPerSecond: order rate limit (disabled if < 0)

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
• logLevel: desired logging level (e.g., "info", "debug")
  • logFile: path to output log file

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Loaded during engine initialization
  • Passed to components like the builder, kill switch, and symbol registry
  • Used to control runtime constraints and outputs

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Engine assumes config is preloaded from JSON or other formats
  • Supports both production and testing environments

docs.title: EventBus
docs.text: EventBus<Event, Policy> is the generic publish/subscribe queue used throughout Flox. It coordinates fan-out of events to multiple listeners with minimal overhead.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
template <typename Event, typename Policy>
class EventBus {
public:
  using Listener = typename ListenerType<Event>::type;
  using Queue = SPSCQueue<typename Policy::QueueItem, 4096>;

  void subscribe(std::shared_ptr<Listener> listener);
  void start();
  void stop();

  void publish(Event ev);
  Queue* getQueue(SubscriberId id);
  uint64_t currentTickId() const;
};

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
• AsyncPolicy – dispatch without waiting. Each event is queued for every subscriber.
  • SyncPolicy – uses a TickBarrier so the publisher waits until all subscribers finish processing.

docs.title: Subscriber Modes
docs.text:
• PUSH – bus spawns a worker thread and pushes events to the listener.
  • PULL – listener calls getQueue() and processes events manually.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Every published event receives an incrementing tick sequence number.
  • Specialized buses (BookUpdateBus, TradeBus, CandleBus, OrderExecutionBus) are typedefs of EventBus with concrete event types.

docs.title: ExchangeConnector
docs.text: The ExchangeConnector class defines an abstract interface for connecting to and receiving data from external trading venues. It serves as the foundation for real or simulated exchange integrations.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide a standard API for: - Subscribing to order book and trade data - Starting/stopping market data streams - Identifying the source of data

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class ExchangeConnector {
public:
  virtual ~ExchangeConnector() = default;

  using BookUpdateCallback = std::function<void(const BookUpdate &)>;
  using TradeCallback = std::function<void(const Trade &)>;

  virtual void start() = 0;
  virtual void stop() = 0;

  virtual std::string exchangeId() const = 0;

  virtual void setCallbacks(BookUpdateCallback onBookUpdate,
                            TradeCallback onTrade);
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Implements start() and stop() lifecycle for data streaming
  • Delivers BookUpdate and Trade data via registered callbacks
  • Identifies itself via exchangeId()

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• Subclasses (e.g. BybitConnector, MockConnector) override the abstract methods
  • setCallbacks() connects internal emitters to external handlers
  • Use emitBookUpdate(...) and emitTrade(...) to dispatch data

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Callbacks are optional but should be set before start()
  • Thread safety and reconnect logic are the responsibility of the derived class

docs.title: ExecutionTrackerAdapter
docs.text: The ExecutionTrackerAdapter bridges the IOrderExecutionListener and IExecutionTracker interfaces. It allows components to track execution latency without modifying their existing callback chains.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To inject latency tracking into systems that already rely on IOrderExecutionListener without duplicating logic.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class ExecutionTrackerAdapter : public IOrderExecutionListener {
public:
  ExecutionTrackerAdapter(SubscriberId id, IExecutionTracker *tracker);

  void onOrderAccepted(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderPartiallyFilled(const Order &order, Quantity qty) override;
  void onOrderFilled(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderCanceled(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderExpired(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderRejected(const Order &order, const std::string &reason) override;
  void onOrderReplaced(const Order &oldOrder, const Order &newOrder) override;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Converts order lifecycle callbacks into IExecutionTracker calls
  • Automatically attaches a timestamp using std::chrono::steady_clock::now()
  • Forwards execution events with timing metadata

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Wrap a PositionManager with tracking support
  • Enable runtime profiling of order latency without coupling strategy code to timing
  • Reuse tracker logic across multiple listeners

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The adapter does nothing if _tracker is nullptr
  • All timestamps are taken at the moment the event is received

docs.title: FullOrderBook
docs.text: The FullOrderBook is a concrete implementation of IOrderBook that maintains the complete bid and ask depth using sorted maps. It is designed for accuracy and completeness over performance.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To track the full state of an order book for a given symbol, including all price levels.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class FullOrderBook : public IOrderBook {
public:
  void applyBookUpdate(const BookUpdate &update) override;
  std::optional<double> bestBid() const override;
  std::optional<double> bestAsk() const override;

  double bidAtPrice(double price) const override;
  double askAtPrice(double price) const override;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Applies full snapshots and delta updates to the book
  • Stores bid levels in descending order using std::map<double, double, std::greater<>>
  • Stores ask levels in ascending order using std::map<double, double>
  • Provides best bid/ask prices and quantity at any given level

docs.title: Thread Safety
docs.text:
• All access and mutation is guarded by a std::mutex
  • Safe for multi-threaded reading/writing

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Historical replay or data capture
  • Backtesting with full book state
  • Accuracy-sensitive trading logic or visualization

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Slower than windowed or top-of-book variants
  • Designed for fidelity and simplicity, not speed

docs.title: Market Data Bus
docs.text: The MarketDataBus is a high-performance event distribution system used to fan out market data (e.g. book updates, trades) to multiple subscribers.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To asynchronously or synchronously deliver events to strategies, aggregators, and books in an efficient, non-blocking, and allocation-free manner.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class MarketDataBus {
public:
  using Queue = BookUpdateBus::Queue;

  void subscribe(std::shared_ptr<IMarketDataSubscriber> subscriber);
  Queue* getQueue(SubscriberId id);

  void publish(EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent> ev);
  void publish(const TradeEvent& ev);

  void start();
  void stop();
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Maintain a dedicated SPSCQueue for each subscriber
  • Deliver events with zero heap allocation
  • Track subscribers by SubscriberId
  • Use EventHandle to manage lifecycle of dispatched events
  • Fan-out book and trade events via internal buses

docs.title: Modes
docs.text:
• Asynchronous (default): Fan-out using lock-free queues
  • Synchronous (USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS): Waits for all subscribers to complete tick processing via TickBarrier

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Internally composed of BookUpdateBus and TradeBus
  • Compatible with both PUSH and PULL subscriber modes
  • Central to real-time book and trade distribution

docs.title: Market Data Event Interface
docs.text: The IMarketDataEvent interface defines the base class for all market data events in Flox, such as book updates, trades, and candles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide a common, pool-aware, and type-safe interface for delivering and recycling events across the system.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class IMarketDataEvent : public RefCountable {
public:
  virtual ~IMarketDataEvent() = default;

  void setPool(IEventPool *pool);
  virtual void releaseToPool();
  virtual void clear();
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Provide a common base for all market data events
  • Participate in pooled memory management via IEventPool

docs.title: Pooling & Lifecycle
docs.text:
• Inherits from RefCountable to track references
  • Implements releaseToPool() to return itself to the originating pool

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All concrete event types like BookUpdateEvent or TradeEvent inherit from this
  • Core to Flox's zero-allocation, high-throughput architecture

docs.title: Event Handle & Event Pool
docs.text: The EventHandle and EventPool classes together manage the lifecycle of market data events in a high-performance, allocation-free manner.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• EventPool<T>: Provides pre-allocated, recyclable instances of event objects (like BookUpdateEvent)
  • EventHandle<T>: Manages scoped ownership of an event, automatically returning it to the pool

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
template <typename EventT>
class EventHandle {
  EventT* get() const;
  EventT* operator->() const;
  EventT& operator*() const;
  operator bool() const;
  template <typename U> EventHandle<U> upcast() const;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Retain ownership of a pooled event
  • Return event to the pool automatically via release()
  • Prevent double-free via RAII
  • Enable upcasting to base types like IMarketDataEvent

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
template <typename EventT, size_t Capacity>
class EventPool : public IEventPool {
  EventHandle<EventT> acquire();
  void release(IMarketDataEvent* event) override;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Pre-allocate a fixed-capacity pool of EventT using aligned storage
  • Provide fast lock-free reuse with SPSCQueue
  • Track acquired and released counts

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used in MarketDataBus and MarketDataEventPool
  • Ensures zero allocations during event fan-out
  • Highly efficient for HFT workloads due to memory locality and lock-freedom

docs.title: MultiExecutionListener
docs.text: The MultiExecutionListener is a fan-out dispatcher for execution events. It enables multiple IOrderExecutionListener consumers to receive notifications for the same order events.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To allow multiple subsystems (e.g., position tracking, analytics, risk) to simultaneously observe order fills and rejections.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class MultiExecutionListener : public IOrderExecutionListener {
public:
  void addListener(IOrderExecutionListener *listener);

  void onOrderFilled(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderRejected(const Order &order, const std::string &reason) override;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Aggregates multiple execution listeners
  • Broadcasts onOrderFilled and onOrderRejected events to all registered listeners
  • Avoids duplicate registration

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Forward execution events to both:
  • PositionManager (for state updates)
  • ExecutionTracker (for latency tracking)
  • Composite behavior in modular systems
  • Strategy instrumentation for backtesting or debugging

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Listeners are stored as raw pointers (ownership not assumed)
  • Safe from duplicate registration via internal check

docs.title: Order
docs.text: The Order struct represents a trade order in the Flox framework. It encapsulates direction, size, price, type, and timestamp for execution tracking and matching.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To serve as the fundamental unit of interaction between strategies, executors, and tracking components.

docs.title: Struct Definition
docs.text:
struct Order {
  uint64_t id;
  Side side;
  double price;
  double quantity;
  OrderType type;
  SymbolId symbol;
  std::chrono::system_clock::time_point timestamp;
};

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
• id: Unique identifier for the order
  • side: Direction of the order (BUY or SELL)
  • price: Price at which to buy/sell (ignored for market orders)
  • quantity: Quantity to be traded
  • type: Order type (LIMIT or MARKET)
  • symbol: Symbol associated with the order
  • timestamp: Time of order creation or submission

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Passed from strategy to order executor
  • Tracked by position and latency components
  • Logged and serialized for auditing or backtesting

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• id must be generated externally and be unique
  • For market orders, price can be set to 0.0 or ignored depending on executor logic

docs.title: OrderExecutionBus
docs.text: OrderExecutionBus dispatches OrderEvent objects to execution listeners. It provides the same asynchronous or synchronous policies as other buses.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
#ifdef USE_SYNC_ORDER_BUS
using OrderExecutionBus = EventBus<OrderEvent, SyncPolicy<OrderEvent>>;
#else
using OrderExecutionBus = EventBus<OrderEvent, AsyncPolicy<OrderEvent>>;
#endif

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Fan-out order lifecycle events (fills, rejections, cancellations)
  • Allow multiple listeners such as PositionManager and latency trackers

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used directly by executors to report order status
  • Controlled by the USE_SYNC_ORDER_BUS compile definition

docs.title: Position Manager
docs.text: The PositionManager tracks and updates symbol-level positions in response to order fills and rejections. It also serves as an execution listener and integrates as a subsystem in the Flox framework.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Maintains internal positions per symbol using fixed-point arithmetic
  • Reacts to order fills and rejections via the IOrderExecutionListener interface
  • Supports querying of position sizes for individual symbols
  • Implements ISubsystem for lifecycle integration
  • Optional component (not strictly required for engine operation)

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class PositionManager : public IPositionManager,
                        public IOrderExecutionListener,
                        public ISubsystem {
public:
  void start() override;
  void stop() override;

  void onOrderFilled(const Order &order) override;
  void onOrderRejected(const Order &order, const std::string &reason) override;

  double getPosition(SymbolId symbol) const override;
  void printPositions() const;
};

docs.title: Internal Representation
docs.text:
• Positions are stored as integers with fixed precision (QTY_PRECISION = 1,000,000) for performance and consistency
  • Uses a static vector of size MAX_SYMBOLS = 65,536 indexed by SymbolId
  • toInternal(double qty) and toDisplay(int64_t qty) are used to convert quantities

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • PositionManager is optional. If your system does not require tracking net positions, this component can be omitted.

docs.title: RefCountable
docs.text: The RefCountable class provides a lightweight reference counting mechanism for pooled objects.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To enable safe and deterministic memory reuse of shared objects (such as events) in a high-performance, low-latency environment.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class RefCountable {
public:
  void retain() noexcept;
  bool release() noexcept;
  void resetRefCount(uint32_t value = 1) noexcept;
  uint32_t refCount() const noexcept;

protected:
  std::atomic<uint32_t> _refCount{0};
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Track ownership count for pooled resources
  • Automatically indicate when an object can be recycled
  • Prevent premature release or double-free via assertions

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• Inherited by IMarketDataEvent
  • Used implicitly by EventHandle to manage scoped lifecycle

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Not thread-safe for shared access — designed for single-threaded ownership transfers
  • Used in conjunction with EventPool and MarketDataBus

docs.title: SPSCQueue
docs.text: The SPSCQueue is a lock-free, single-producer single-consumer queue optimized for high-throughput, low-latency communication.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide efficient inter-thread message passing without dynamic memory allocation or locks.

docs.title: Template Parameters
docs.text:
template <typename T, size_t Capacity>
class SPSCQueue;
  • T: Type of element (must be nothrow destructible)
  • Capacity: Power-of-two queue size

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
bool push(const T &item);
bool emplace(T &&item);
template <typename... Args> bool try_emplace(Args&&...);
bool pop(T &out);
T* try_pop();
std::optional<std::reference_wrapper<T>> try_pop_ref();
bool empty() const;
bool full() const;
size_t size() const;

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Store a bounded ring buffer of T
  • Allow single-producer push and single-consumer pop without contention
  • Maintain high cache locality and eliminate allocations

docs.title: Internals
docs.text:
• Based on a circular buffer
  • _head and _tail are padded to avoid false sharing
  • Uses std::aligned_storage_t to store elements without constructing them prematurely

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Capacity must be a power of two
  • Used internally in MarketDataBus to deliver events to each subscriber
  • Destructs all remaining elements on shutdown

docs.title: Example Use
docs.text:
SPSCQueue<int, 1024> queue;
queue.push(42);
int x;
if (queue.pop(x)) { /* use x */ }

docs.title: StorageSink
docs.text: The StorageSink interface defines a generic sink for storing executed orders. It is part of the Flox engine’s persistence and logging infrastructure.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To persist orders for later analysis, backtesting, compliance, or monitoring.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class StorageSink : public ISubsystem {
public:
  virtual ~StorageSink() = default;
  virtual void store(const Order &order) = 0;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Accepts Order objects via store(...)
  • Implements storage logic (e.g. MongoDB, file-based, in-memory)
  • Acts as a pluggable subsystem

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Write trades to a database for historical analysis
  • Stream orders to a file or external telemetry system
  • Capture order flow for backtesting or compliance

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Must be connected to execution callbacks (e.g., via IOrderExecutionListener)
  • Storage behavior is defined by derived implementations

docs.title:
ISubsystem and Subsystem

The ISubsystem interface represents a component with a well-defined start/stop lifecycle. Subsystem<T> is a generic wrapper that adapts any compatible component to conform to ISubsystem.
Purpose
docs.text: To enable modular lifecycle management of engine components.

docs.title: Interface Definition
docs.text:
class ISubsystem {
public:
  virtual ~ISubsystem() = default;
  virtual void start() = 0;
  virtual void stop() = 0;
};

docs.title: Template Wrapper
docs.text:
template <typename T>
class Subsystem : public ISubsystem {
public:
  explicit Subsystem(std::unique_ptr<T> impl);

  void start() override;
  void stop() override;

  T *get() const;
};

docs.title: ISubsystem
docs.text:
• Defines the basic start/stop interface for engine-managed components
  • Ensures consistent lifecycle control for execution, routing, logging, etc.

docs.title: Subsystem<T>
docs.text:
• Adapts any class with start() and stop() methods to conform to ISubsystem
  • Uses compile-time check (if constexpr) to guard optional method presence
  • Owns the underlying component via std::unique_ptr

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Strategy managers, risk modules, sinks, routers — all can implement or be wrapped as subsystems
  • Managed startup/shutdown via engine orchestration

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Subsystem<T> allows non-virtual classes to be managed uniformly
  • Wrappers ensure loosely coupled lifecycle coordination

docs.title: SymbolRegistry
docs.text: The SymbolRegistry provides a compact mapping between string-based exchange:symbol names and internal SymbolId integers. It supports efficient registration, lookup, and reverse resolution of trading symbols.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To assign and maintain stable, compact SymbolId values for all exchange-symbol pairs.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class SymbolRegistry {
public:
  SymbolId registerSymbol(const std::string &exchange,
                          const std::string &symbol);

  std::optional<SymbolId> getSymbolId(const std::string &exchange,
                                      const std::string &symbol) const;

  std::pair<std::string, std::string> getSymbolName(SymbolId id) const;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Registers symbols and assigns a unique SymbolId per (exchange, symbol) pair
  • Supports reverse lookup of exchange/symbol strings by ID
  • Ensures thread-safe access via std::mutex

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Central registry used across the engine for symbol resolution
  • Used by routers, strategies, order books, and logging
  • Enables fast ID-based access instead of string comparisons

docs.title: Internal Design
docs.text:
• _map: maps "exchange:symbol" to SymbolId
  • _reverse: vector of pairs for reverse mapping by index
  • _mutex: protects access to shared state

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Once registered, symbol IDs remain stable
  • Registry must be prepopulated at engine startup or config loading phase

docs.title: Tick Barrier
docs.text: The TickBarrier is a synchronization primitive used to coordinate multiple subscribers during synchronous market data processing.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To ensure all subscribers complete their tick before the next tick is published, enabling fully deterministic event handling.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class TickBarrier {
public:
  explicit TickBarrier(size_t total);
  void complete();
  void wait();
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Track the number of subscribers that have completed processing
  • Block until all subscribers signal completion of the current tick

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Used in SyncMarketDataBus when USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS is defined:

barrier.complete(); // Subscriber signals done
barrier.wait();     // Publisher waits for all to complete

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Thread-safe via atomic counter
  • Uses spin-waiting (yield) to avoid context switches
  • Should only be used in low-latency, simulation, or testing environments

docs.title: Tick Guard
docs.text: The TickGuard is an RAII utility that automatically signals tick completion when it goes out of scope.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Simplify synchronization logic in MarketDataBus (synchronized version) by ensuring each subscriber signals its completion through the TickBarrier.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
class TickGuard {
public:
  explicit TickGuard(TickBarrier &barrier);
  ~TickGuard();

  TickGuard(const TickGuard &) = delete;
  TickGuard &operator=(const TickGuard &) = delete;
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Call barrier.complete() automatically on destruction
  • Prevent missing tick completions due to early returns or exceptions

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent& ev) {
  TickGuard guard(barrier);
  // ... process tick ...
} // completion is signaled automatically

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used in synchronous market data processing mode
  • Should only be constructed with a valid reference to TickBarrier

docs.title: TradeBus
docs.text: TradeBus distributes TradeEvent objects to subscribers. It is implemented via the generic EventBus template.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
#ifdef USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS
using TradeBus = EventBus<TradeEvent, SyncPolicy<TradeEvent>>;
#else
using TradeBus = EventBus<TradeEvent, AsyncPolicy<TradeEvent>>;
#endif

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Broadcast trades to strategies and analytics modules
  • Provide sequence numbering when publishing

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Combined with BookUpdateBus in MarketDataBus
  • Controlled by the USE_SYNC_MARKET_BUS compile definition

docs.title: Trade Event
docs.text: The TradeEvent represents an executed trade in the market, including direction, price, and quantity.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To provide subscribers (e.g., strategies, volume analyzers) with real-time trade information in a zero-allocation, poolable format.

docs.title: Interface Summary
docs.text:
struct TradeEvent : public IMarketDataEvent {
  SymbolId symbol;
  double price;
  double quantity;
  bool isBuy;
  std::chrono::system_clock::time_point timestamp;

  TradeEvent(std::pmr::memory_resource *);
};

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Represent a single trade tick
  • Indicate direction with isBuy
  • Delivered to subscribers via the trade bus

docs.title: Memory Optimization
docs.text:
• Accepts a polymorphic memory resource for consistency with pooled event design
  • Recycled via EventPool<TradeEvent>

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Used by latency-sensitive strategies for trade confirmation
  • Supports precise event timestamps for time-based logic

docs.title: WindowedOrderBook
docs.text: WindowedOrderBook is a high-performance implementation of IOrderBook that maintains a centered price window of limited depth. It is optimized for speed, memory efficiency, and cache locality in latency-sensitive environments.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To track a limited range of price levels around the center price, making it ideal for real-time trading systems.

docs.title: Class Definition
docs.text:
class WindowedOrderBook : public IOrderBook {
public:
  WindowedOrderBook(double tickSize, double expectedDeviation,
                    std::pmr::memory_resource *mem);

  void applyBookUpdate(const BookUpdate &update) override;

  size_t priceToIndex(double price) const;
  double indexToPrice(size_t index) const;
  bool isPriceInWindow(double price) const;

  double bidAtPrice(double price) const override;
  double askAtPrice(double price) const override;

  std::optional<double> bestBid() const override;
  std::optional<double> bestAsk() const override;

  double getBidQuantity(double price) const;
  double getAskQuantity(double price) const;

  void printBook(std::size_t depth = 10) const;
  double centerPrice() const;
};

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Maintains a fixed-size ring buffer of price levels centered around _centerPrice
  • Bid and ask sides are implemented using BookSide
  • tickSize and expectedDeviation define price granularity and window size

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Fast lookup and updates via index mapping (priceToIndex)
  • Memory-local storage for high CPU cache efficiency
  • Thread-safe via internal std::mutex

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Used in live strategies where full book depth is unnecessary
  • Engine-level real-time book tracking and signal generation
  • Suitable for DLOB-style matching and top-of-book decision logic

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Incoming book updates outside the window trigger shiftWindow(...)
  • Prices must be aligned to tick size for correctness

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Flox is configured via the EngineConfig structure, typically loaded from a file.

docs.title: Example (JSON-like)
docs.text:
{
  "logLevel": "debug",
  "exchanges": [
    {
      "name": "bybit",
      "type": "mock",
      "symbols": [
        { "symbol": "DOTUSDT", "tickSize": 0.001, "expectedDeviation": 0.5 }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "killSwitchConfig": {
    "maxOrderQty": 10000,
    "maxLoss": -5000,
    "maxOrdersPerSecond": 100
  }
}

docs.title: Demo Application
docs.text: The demo folder contains a minimal example showing how to wire Flox components together. It assembles a working engine with simulated market data and a very simple trading strategy.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• DemoConnector generates synthetic trades and book updates
  • DemoStrategy reacts to trades and updates its own order book
  • SimpleOrderExecutor submits mock orders and reports fills via OrderExecutionBus
  • SimplePnLTracker, SimpleKillSwitch, and SimpleRiskManager illustrate supporting modules
  • DemoBuilder creates MarketDataBus, CandleBus, and other subsystems

docs.title: Running
docs.text:
After building the project with CMake you can run the demo binary:

./demo/flox_demo

It will start two demo connectors, publish market data, and run the strategy for around five seconds before stopping.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide helps you build and install Flox on your machine.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• C++20 or later (e.g., GCC 13)
  • CMake 3.22+
  • Git
  • Linux (recommended)
  • GoogleTest and Google Benchmark
  • clang-format 18.1.8 (for development and contribution)

docs.title: Clone and Build
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/eeiaao/flox.git
cd flox
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..
make -j$(nproc)

docs.title: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
If gtest and benchmark are not already available system-wide, you can install them manually:

# GoogleTest
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/google/googletest.git
cmake -B gtest-build -S googletest
cmake --build gtest-build --target gtest gtest_main gmock gmock_main
sudo cmake --install gtest-build

# Google Benchmark
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/google/benchmark.git
cmake -B benchmark-build -S benchmark -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBENCHMARK_DOWNLOAD_DEPENDENCIES=ON
cmake --build benchmark-build -j$(nproc)
sudo cmake --install benchmark-build

If you already have them installed via your package manager, you can skip this step.

CI uses clang-format 18 to ensure formatting. For clang-format version matters (!). To install it locally:

sudo apt install -y wget gnupg lsb-release software-properties-common
wget https://apt.llvm.org/llvm.sh
chmod +x llvm.sh
sudo ./llvm.sh 18
sudo apt install -y clang-format-18
sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/clang-format clang-format /usr/bin/clang-format-18 100

docs.title: Custom Build Options
docs.text:
Flox supports optional components controlled via CMake options:

  • ENABLE_TESTS (default: ON) – build unit tests
  • ENABLE_BENCHMARK (default: ON) – build performance benchmarks

You can disable them like so:

cmake .. -DENABLE_TESTS=OFF -DENABLE_BENCHMARK=OFF

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text:
From the build directory:

ctest --output-on-failure

docs.title: Run Benchmarks
docs.text:
From the build directory:

./benchmarks/full_order_book_benchmark

You can also run any other benchmark binary in the benchmarks/ directory.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
To install the library system-wide:

sudo make install

docs.title: Contribution
docs.text:
All contributions are welcome via pull requests.

Please follow the existing project structure and naming conventions. Add tests, benchmarks, and documentation where appropriate. We use clang-format for code style, the .clang-format file is provided in the project root.

A pre-commit hook that auto-formats changed .cpp and .h files is automatically installed when you run cmake .. (enabled by default). It will copy scripts/pre-commit into .git/hooks/pre-commit.

If needed, you can also install it manually:

cp scripts/pre-commit .git/hooks/pre-commit
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

docs.title: Use in Your Project
docs.text: Flox is a collection of reusable low-latency building blocks. You can use it as a library for developing your own HFT engine or trading infrastructure.

docs.title: Running the Flox Engine
docs.text: This guide explains how to initialize and run the Flox engine using a custom IEngineBuilder implementation.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
To launch the engine:

  1. Implement the IEngineBuilder interface
  2. Register subsystems, connectors, and strategies
  3. Return a fully initialized IEngine instance (typically Engine)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Here’s a simplified example using DemoEngineBuilder:

class DemoEngineBuilder : public IEngineBuilder {
public:
  DemoEngineBuilder(const EngineConfig &config) : _config(config) {}

  std::unique_ptr<IEngine> build() override {
    auto registry = std::make_unique<SymbolRegistry>();
    auto mdb = std::make_unique<MarketDataBus>();
    auto orderBus = std::make_unique<OrderExecutionBus>();

    // Register connectors
    ConnectorFactory::instance().registerConnector("bybit",
      [mdb = mdb.get(), registry = registry.get()](const std::string &symbolStr) {
        auto symbolId = registry->getSymbolId("bybit", symbolStr);
        auto conn = std::make_shared<BybitExchangeConnector>(symbolStr, *symbolId);
        conn->setCallbacks(
          [mdb](EventHandle<BookUpdateEvent> b) { mdb->publish(std::move(b)); },
          [mdb](const TradeEvent &t) { mdb->publish(t); });
        return conn;
      });

    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<ExchangeConnector>> connectors;
    std::vector<std::unique_ptr<ISubsystem>> subsystems;

    // Symbol registration
    for (const auto &ex : _config.exchanges) {
      for (const auto &sym : ex.symbols) {
        registry->registerSymbol(ex.name, sym.symbol);
        auto conn = ConnectorFactory::instance().createConnector(ex.name, sym.symbol);
        if (conn) connectors.push_back(conn);
      }
    }

    // Strategy wiring
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<IStrategy>> strategies =
        loadStrategiesFromConfig(registry.get());

    for (const auto &strat : strategies) {
      auto executor = std::make_unique<SimpleOrderExecutor>(*orderBus);
      strat->setOrderExecutor(executor.get());
      subsystems.push_back(std::move(executor));
      mdb->subscribe(strat);
    }

    // Subsystem wiring
    subsystems.push_back(std::move(mdb));
    subsystems.push_back(std::move(orderBus));
    subsystems.push_back(std::make_unique<Subsystem<SymbolRegistry>>(std::move(registry)));

    return std::make_unique<Engine>(_config, std::move(subsystems), std::move(connectors));
  }

private:
  EngineConfig _config;
};

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Strategies must implement IMarketDataSubscriber
  • Use Subsystem<T> to integrate non-ISubsystem modules
  • Event pools ensure events are dispatched without allocations
  • To run the engine:
auto engine = builder.build();
engine->start();

Use this pattern to dynamically configure symbols, connectors, and strategies at runtime.

docs.title: Writing Strategies
docs.text: Strategies in Flox are implemented by subclassing IStrategy, which defines a uniform interface for receiving market data and submitting orders. Strategies are market data subscribers with execution capability and dependency injection.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: To encapsulate trading logic that reacts to real-time or historical market data, and interfaces with execution, validation, position, and risk systems.

docs.title: Interface Overview
docs.text:
class IStrategy : public IMarketDataSubscriber {
 public:
  // Lifecycle
  virtual void onStart();
  virtual void onStop();

  // Market data event callbacks
  virtual void onCandle(const CandleEvent& candle) override;
  virtual void onTrade(const TradeEvent& trade) override;
  virtual void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent& bookUpdate) override;

  // MarketDataSubscriber identification
  SubscriberId id() const override;
  SubscriberMode mode() const override;

  // Dependencies
  void setRiskManager(IRiskManager*);
  void setPositionManager(IPositionManager*);
  void setOrderExecutor(IOrderExecutor*);
  void setOrderValidator(IOrderValidator*);
  void setKillSwitch(IKillSwitch*);

 protected:
  IOrderExecutor* GetOrderExecutor() const;
  IRiskManager* GetRiskManager() const;
  IPositionManager* GetPositionManager() const;
  IOrderValidator* GetOrderValidator() const;
  IKillSwitch* GetKillSwitch() const;
};

docs.title: Strategy Lifecycle
docs.text:
• onStart() — called by engine on startup (before any events)
  • onStop() — called before engine shutdown

docs.title: Market Data Subscriptions
docs.text:
Each strategy is a IMarketDataSubscriber and automatically receives: - onBookUpdate(...) - onTrade(...) - onCandle(...)

depending on subscription mode (PUSH or PULL). Strategies explicitly define their pull or push behavior via SubscriberMode. Synchronous mode (enabled via compile-time flag) deliver events deterministically, but strategy logic remains the same across environments. Pull mode can be used in live mode as well (e.g., ML strategies).

docs.title: Order Submission Flow
docs.text:
Use the injected components to guard order flow:

if (GetOrderValidator() && !GetOrderValidator()->validate(order, reason)) return;
if (GetRiskManager() && !GetRiskManager()->allow(order)) return;
GetOrderExecutor()->submitOrder(order);

This ensures: - Pre-check with validator - Risk compliance check - Order is submitted only when both checks pass

docs.title: Dependency Injection
docs.text: Set during initialization via: - setOrderExecutor() — required, routes orders - setRiskManager() — optional, risk filter - setPositionManager() — optional, local position view - setOrderValidator() — optional, stateless pre-check - setKillSwitch() — optional, emergency stop integration

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep all callbacks (onTrade, etc) non-blocking — each subscriber runs in its own thread with a dedicated queue, so one strategy cannot block others. However, minimizing latency is still important for responsiveness and scheduling fairness
  • Avoid allocations and blocking I/O
  • Track only minimal state per-symbol or per-tick
  • Never retain raw pointers to event objects — they may be returned to a pool and reused after the callback returns
  • Only use PositionManager if your strategy requires tracking positions internally. Stateless strategies or pure signal generators often don't need it and can avoid unnecessary dependencies

docs.title: Example Strategy
docs.text:
class MyStrategy : public IStrategy {
public:
  void onBookUpdate(const BookUpdateEvent& update) override {
    if (!shouldEnter(update)) return;

    Order order = buildOrder(update);
    auto* validator = GetOrderValidator();
    auto* risk = GetRiskManager();
    auto* executor = GetOrderExecutor();

    std::string reason;
    if (validator && !validator->validate(order, reason)) return;
    if (risk && !risk->allow(order)) return;
    executor->submitOrder(order);
  }

private:
  bool shouldEnter(const BookUpdateEvent& update) const {
    // Simple trigger: spread is wide enough
    Price spread = update.bestAskPrice() - update.bestBidPrice();
    return spread >= MinSpread;
  }

  Order buildOrder(const BookUpdateEvent& update) const {
    // Attempt to enter passively near best bid
    Price price = update.bestBidPrice() + TickImprovement;
    Quantity qty = DefaultQuantity;
    return Order{/* symbol */ update.symbol(),
                 /* side */ Side::Buy,
                 /* price */ price,
                 /* qty */ qty,
                 /* type */ OrderType::Limit};
  }

  static constexpr Price TickImprovement = 0.01;
  static constexpr Price MinSpread = 0.03;
  static constexpr Quantity DefaultQuantity = 100;
};

docs.title: Integration with Engine
docs.text: Strategies are registered via factory mechanisms (e.g. StrategyConfig) and assigned to specific symbols. Strategies operate identically in both live and backtest modes via unified interface.
